coredata.dc:description: The corruption of refrigerated marine fish results in global economic losses exceeding 25 billion euros annually. However, conventional preservatives present challenges, including singular functionality, potential toxicity, and high cost. In response, we developed multifunctional, safe, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly carbon dots derived from radish residues (R-CDs) by using the one-pot hydrothermal method. The surface of R-CDs is enriched with hydroxyl groups, conferring broad-spectrum antioxidant and antibacterial characteristics. R-CDs exhibited a notable 72.92 % inhibition rate on lipid peroxidation, surpassing the effectiveness of vitamin C (46 %). Additionally, R-CDs demonstrated impressive scavenging rates of 93.8 % for 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radicals and 99.36 % for 2,2-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid-free radicals. In combating spoilage bacteria such as Aeromonas sobria and Hafnia alvei, R-CDs disrupted cell structures and influenced intracellular substance content. Importantly, co-cultivation with R-CDs showed no significant cytotoxicity. Further incorporating R-CDs into films using starch and chitosan (S/CS/R-CDs films) for efficient and convenient use in salmon fillets preservation. S/CS/R-CDs films effectively inhibited the growth of spoilage bacteria, lipid oxidation, and protein decomposition in salmon fillets, thereby extending shelf life by 4 days. This combination of antioxidant and antibacterial properties in R-CDs, along with the functional films, presents a promising approach for enhancing salmon fillet preservation.

head.ce:title: Radish residue carbon dots-based novel starch/chitosan film with high antioxidant, biocompatibility, and antibacterial activities for salmon fillets' active packaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The corruption of refrigerated marine fish results in global economic losses exceeding 25 billion euros annually. However, conventional preservatives present challenges, including singular functionality, potential toxicity, and high cost. In response, we developed multifunctional, safe, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly carbon dots derived from radish residues (R-CDs) by using the one-pot hydrothermal method. The surface of R-CDs is enriched with hydroxyl groups, conferring broad-spectrum antioxidant and antibacterial characteristics. R-CDs exhibited a notable 72.92 % inhibition rate on lipid peroxidation, surpassing the effectiveness of vitamin C (46 %). Additionally, R-CDs demonstrated impressive scavenging rates of 93.8 % for 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radicals and 99.36 % for 2,2-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid-free radicals. In combating spoilage bacteria such as Aeromonas sobria and Hafnia alvei, R-CDs disrupted cell structures and influenced intracellular substance content. Importantly, co-cultivation with R-CDs showed no significant cytotoxicity. Further incorporating R-CDs into films using starch and chitosan (S/CS/R-CDs films) for efficient and convenient use in salmon fillets preservation. S/CS/R-CDs films effectively inhibited the growth of spoilage bacteria, lipid oxidation, and protein decomposition in salmon fillets, thereby extending shelf life by 4 days. This combination of antioxidant and antibacterial properties in R-CDs, along with the functional films, presents a promising approach for enhancing salmon fillet preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic products, valued for their unique freshness and nutritional richness, provide essential nutrients such as protein, unsaturated fatty acids, and vitamins to a wide consumer base [1,2]. However, the high nutrient and water content inherent in aquatic products makes them susceptible to microbial contamination and various biochemical reactions, leading to a decline in sensory and nutritional quality [3]. The corruption of refrigerated marine fish causes global economic losses, exceeding 25 billion euros annually [4]. Despite the application of various preservation methods, including preservatives such as essential oils [5], polyphenols [6–8], ultrasound [9], plasma [10], electron beam irradiation [11], and vacuum packaging [12], the existing preservation technologies face challenges related to inefficiency and high costs. Consequently, there is a growing interest in exploring more effective and affordable preservation methods and technologies.

Active packaging films, capable of inhibiting oxidation and bacterial growth by releasing active substances within the packaging material, represent a promising avenue for improving preservation [13,14]. Starch (S) and chitosan (CS), commonly used carrier matrices derived from natural and degradable sources, are frequently employed in membrane formation. S, owing to its abundant sources, ease of film formation, low cost, and safety, is a widely utilized membrane material [15]. However, challenges such as low hydrophobicity and suboptimal mechanical and vapor barrier properties limit the application of S-based packaging films. The incorporation of CS not only enhances the mechanical and barrier properties of S-based films but also imparts antibacterial properties, making them suitable for use as antibacterial films [13].

Nanomaterials have emerged as a compelling area of study in the development of antibacterial additives. Nanoparticle-rich films have been reported to be superior to conventional packaging materials in the preservation of aquatic products. Various reports have revealed that the addition of nanomaterials such as zinc oxide or silver nanoparticles to the film can enhance film performance; however, these nanoparticles exhibit certain limitations with regard to safety and film compatibility [16,17]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been introduced as novel nanomaterials exhibiting photoluminescent properties with small particle sizes [18,19]. They possess surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups, exhibiting excellent functional properties including luminescence and antibacterial and antioxidant efficacies [20,21]. Biomass waste, recognized for its safety, environmental friendliness, and abundant, low-cost renewable raw material sources, offers an attractive option for producing high biosafety CDs [22]. Biomass-based CDs have demonstrated the potential to develop new active packaging with multiple functions such as antibacterial and antioxidant properties [23]. Thus, the combination of CS and S with CDs is expected to yield functional films with improved physical properties for active packaging applications.

This study focuses on the preparation of radish residue-derived CDs (R-CDs) with demonstrated antibacterial and antioxidant properties. The broad-spectrum antibacterial activities of R-CDs were assessed, and their antioxidant capacity was evaluated using the HEK-293 cell model. Changes in intracellular superoxide dismutase (SOD), malondialdehyde (MDA), and glutathione (GSH) contents were explored to unveil the protective mechanism of R-CDs against oxidative damage to cells. Furthermore, S/CS composite preservation films loaded with R-CDs (S/CS/R-CDs) were prepared, and the antibacterial and antioxidant activities of S/CS/R-CDs films were investigated. Finally, the practical application of S/CS/R-CDs films was verified through salmon fillet coating. Overall, this study provides a potential approach to designing new CD polysaccharide-based films for seafood preservation.

White radish and maize S were procured from Jinzhou RT-MART, Liaoning, China. CS and 2,2-azino-bis-3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid (ABTS) were obtained from Macklin Reagent Corp (Shanghai, China). The ultramicro ATP enzyme (Na+ K+) test kit, Alkaline Phosphatase (AKP) Test Kit, SOD detection kit, MDA detection kit, and GSH detection kit were sourced from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). Active oxygen detection kits and Ezup column bacterial genomic DNA extraction kits were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corp (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

Bacillus licheniformis, Serratia marcescens, Hafnia alvei, Listeria monocytogenes, Aeromonas sobria, Pseudomonas fluorescens, and Pseudomonas fragi were cultured in Luria–Bertani (LB) broth at 160 rpm and 28 °C. Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were cultured in LB broth at 160 rpm and temperatures of 37 °C. The strains were supplied by the Key Laboratory of Food Safety of Bohai University.

R-CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method. Radish residue was obtained by mashing discarded radishes after experimentation. A total of 2.00 g of radish residue was combined with 20.00 mL of deionized water and then transferred to a 25.00 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated at 220 °C for 6 h. After reaching room temperature, the R-CDs were cooled and then centrifuged at 20,000 g for 20 min to separate the supernatant. Dialysis was performed using a 500 Da dialysis membrane for 5 h. The R-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C or freeze-dried for preservation.

The morphology of the samples was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer (Japan) was utilized to analyze the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of R-CDs under the conditions of 40 kV and 50 mA. The chemical composition of the sample was determined using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Scimitar 2000 Near, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by Thermo Escalab 250 XI (USA). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The fluorescence emission spectrum of the sample was obtained using an F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial properties of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of R-CDs against bacteria and the growth curve of A. sobria were determined following a previously reported method [24]. Subsequently, the antibacterial properties of R-CDs were evaluated through live/dead bacteria staining experiments and bacterial morphological studies.

Bacteria in the logarithmic growth phase were centrifuged and resuspended in a sterile 0.85 % NaCl solution (bacterial suspension concentration was 1 × 107 CFU/mL). The suspension was treated with carbon dots at final concentrations of 1/2 MIC (1 mg/mL), 1 MIC (2 mg/mL), and 2 MIC (4 mg/mL), with the control group comprising a bacterial suspension without R-CDs. After incubation at 28 °C for 4 h, the bacterial suspension was washed twice with 0.85 % NaCl solution. Green SYTO9 (1.5 μL) and Propidium Iodide (PI) (1.5 μL) were added to 1.00 mL of the bacterial suspension and cultured for 15 min in the dark. After cleaning with 0.85 % NaCl solution, the bacterial suspension was observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

To further confirm the bactericidal effect of R-CDs, scanning electron microscopy was employed. A. sobria cultured in the logarithmic growth phase (1.0 × 107 CFU/mL) was centrifuged (10,000 g, 10 min) and washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to collect bacteria. The resuspended bacterial solution was mixed with R-CDs solution to final concentrations of 1/2 MIC (1 mg/mL), 1 MIC (2 mg/mL), and 2 MIC (4 mg/mL), with PBS used as a blank control. Following incubation in a shaker for 6 h, the bacterial solution was centrifuged and washed three times with PBS. A 5 mm × 5 mm zinc plate was used as a carrier, fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 4 h, and eluted with ethanol. The dried zinc sheet was gold-sprayed, and cell morphology was observed using scanning electron microscopy.

For the determination of DPPH, 1.00 mL of R-CDs solution was added to 4.00 mL of 0.1 mmol/L DPPH solution (dissolved in 95 % ethanol). The absorbance value (A1) was measured at 517 nm after 30 min of dark reaction. The absorbance value (A2) was measured at 517 nm using 95 % absolute ethanol instead of DPPH solution, and the absorbance value (A0) was measured at 517 nm using distilled water instead of R-CDs. The DPPH-free radical scavenging rate was calculated based on Formula (1).
(1)

For ABTS determination, a 7.00 mmol/L ABTS solution and 2.45 mmol/L potassium persulfate solution were mixed at a 1:1 ratio and placed in the dark at room temperature for 12 h to produce ABTS+. To obtain a working solution, ABTS+ was diluted with PBS until the absorbance was reduced to approximately 0.7. Different concentrations of R-CDs solution (0.5 mL of each) were mixed with the working solution and PBS at 1:10, respectively. The absorbance was measured at 734 nm after standing in the dark for 6 min, recorded as A1 and A2. PBS and ABTS (0.5 mL) were mixed at 1:10, and the absorbance measured under the same conditions was recorded as A0. The ABTS-free radical-scavenging rate was calculated based on Formula (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of cell oxidative damage model
ce:section.ce:para: HEK-293 cells (5.00 × 104 cells/mL) were seeded in 96-well plates. Subsequently, the cells were divided into blank, control, and experimental groups. The blank group comprised cells treated with 100.00 μL of medium only. The control group consisted of cells prepared with 90.00 μL of cell suspension and 10.00 μL of medium, whereas the experimental group comprised 90 μL of cell suspension. After incubation for 24 h, 10.0 μL of different concentrations of H2O2 (200.00, 300.00, 400.00, 500.00, 600.00, 700.00, 800.00, 900.00 μmol/L) was added, and the cells were incubated for an additional 12 h. Cell viability was measured using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method.

HEK-293 cells were transferred to 96-well plates at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells/well and cultured in an incubator (5 % CO2, 37 °C) for 24 h. After discarding the culture medium in the well, 100.00 μL of R-CDs solution was added to the well, and only Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (10 % fetal bovine serum) was added to the blank control group. The cells were cultured for 24 h, and cell viability was measured using the CCK-8 method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effect of R-CDs on H2O2-induced oxidative damage in cells
ce:section.ce:para: HEK-293 cells (1.0 × 105 cells/mL) were inoculated into 96-well plates. The control, injury, and protection groups were inoculated with 100.00 μL of cell suspension. After incubation in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h, the medium was discarded. The control and injury groups were inoculated with 90.0 μL of medium, and the protection group was inoculated with 90.00 μL of medium containing different concentrations (100.00, 200.00, 300.00, 400.00, 500.00, 600.00 μg/mL) of R-CDs for 24 h. Subsequently, the control group was inoculated with 10.00 μL of medium, and the other two groups were inoculated with 10.00 μL of H2O2 solution. Following 12 h of incubation, cell viability, SOD, MDA, and GSH were measured using the CCK-8 method, SOD, MDA, and GSH kits, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of S/CS/R-CD films in salmon fillet preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of S/CS/R-CD films: 10.0 g of starch was added to 5.0 g of glycerol in 160.0 mL of water and then gelatinized at 90 °C for 40 min by magnetic stirring. Then, 6.0 g of chitosan was added to 160.0 mL of 2 % acetic acid solution and completely dissolved by magnetic stirring for 2 h. Then, the chitosan solution and 10.0 mL R-CDs solution with the total mass of chitosan and starch of 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 % were added into the starch solution, respectively. After stirring at 80 °C for 30 min, the solution was removed and subjected to ultrasonic degassing for 2 h after cooling. Finally, the film-forming solution was cast in a 10 × 10-cm2 plastic plate and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 6 h. After drying, the film was removed for use. The films were recorded as S/CS, S/CS/R-CDs-0.5, S/CS/R-CDs-1, and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 in accordance with the concentration of R-CDs.

Purchased fresh salmon fillet pieces were cut into several 100.00 ± 5.00 g salmon fillets in a sterile environment. The film was placed under a UV lamp for 20 min for sterilization. Salmon fillets were randomly divided into four groups: uncoated salmon fillets (control group), polyethylene (PE) film-coated salmon fillets, S/CS film-coated salmon fillets, and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film-coated salmon fillets. The samples were stored at 4 °C. Sensory evaluation, total viable count (TVC), pH, total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), and 2-thiobarbituric acid (TBA) values were measured every 2 days during the 10 days of storage. The salmon fillet samples were stained with hematoxylin and eosin on days 0, 6, and 10.

Each sample was tested three times in parallel, and the results were averaged. Origin 2019 software was used to analyze the data, and SPSS 20.0 software was used for statistical analysis. A significance level of P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant, whereas P > 0.05 indicated no significant difference. Further details of the experiments are described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Small-molecule biomass CDs exhibit excellent biological activity, sparking significant interest in biomass-derived CDs [25]. We used white radish residue as a precursor to synthesize R-CDs with antioxidant and antibacterial properties using a one-pot hydrothermal method (Fig. 1a). The morphology and size of the synthesized R-CDs were characterized using TEM, which showed that the R-CDs were spherical in shape, well-dispersed, and exhibited an average particle diameter of 1.47 ± 0.48 nm. (Fig. 1b). The ultra-small size allows R-CDs to enter the cell through the cell wall and cell membrane, exerting a bactericidal effect. Additionally, HRTEM images of R-CDs revealed lattice stripes with intervals of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100)-crystal plane of graphite (Fig. 1c), confirming that the R-CDs were successfully synthesized [26]. We measured XPS values to verify the chemical elemental composition of the carbon quantum dots. The full spectrum (Fig. 1f) indicated that the R-CDs comprised C, N, and O, with C accounting for 70.3 %, N for 26.2 %, and O for 3.5 %. The refined C1s spectrum revealed peaks at 284.6, 285.6, 286.5, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to the CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1a) [27,28]. The N1s spectrum of the R-CDs showed peaks near 399.8 eV and 401.8 eV, which were associated with NH and CN bonds (Fig. S1b) [29]. The O1s spectra showed signal peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.3 eV, which were in agreement with the CO and C-O/O-H bands (Fig. S1c) [30]. According to the XPS results, the R-CDs were indicated to contain numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, which contributed to good water solubility [31].

XRD images demonstrated that the center position was approximately at 2θ = 21.5°, corresponding to the graphite carbon (002) plane (Fig. 1d). FTIR spectroscopy was used to identify the functional groups present on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 1e). A distinct absorption peak at 3402 cm−1 was attributed to the NH or OH stretching vibration [32]. Furthermore, stretching vibrations of C − H were observed at 2930 cm−1 [33]. Bands observed at 1703, 1600, 1409, 1120, and 1050 cm−1 correspond to CO, CC, -COOH, CN, and CO vibrations, respectively [34,35]. In conclusion, there are abundant hydrophilic groups in R-CDs, contributing to their good water solubility. Furthermore, functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups enable R-CDs to interact with cell membranes and disrupt bacterial structures, indicating their potential for potent antibacterial properties.

The optical properties of R-CDs were investigated. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1g, revealing a solitary absorption peak at 275 nm, attributed to the π-π* electron transition of CC and CN bonds [36]. Under the maximum excitation wavelength of 335 nm, the emission wavelength of R-CDs is 423 nm. Fig. S1d depicts the emission spectra of R-CDs. With an increase in the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peak undergoes a redshift, and the fluorescence intensity initially increases and then decreases, exhibiting excitation dependence. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that R-CD surfaces contain numerous C-, O-, and N-functional groups that can generate various surface states with different energy levels, resulting in a series of emission traps. At different excitation wavelengths, the corresponding surface state emission traps will dominate, leading to excitation-dependent PL emission [37].

The stability of R-CDs has a great influence on its practical application. We evaluated the stability of R-CDs at different time points and pHs. Fig. S2a depicts that the fluorescence intensity of R-CD solution had a slight difference at pH 3–9, but the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly at pH 2 or 10, which may be related to the protonation of amino groups and the deprotonation of carboxyl hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs. Similar results were observed after 21 days of storage (Fig. S2b), indicating that R-CDs possessed good light stability. Thus, R-CD exerts antibacterial and antioxidant activities with a change in time and pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial properties of R-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the antibacterial effects of R-CDs at various concentrations using different aquatic spoilage bacteria as representative strains through the plate counting method (MIC test). As shown in Fig. 2a, with the increase in CD concentrations, the antibacterial effect was progressively enhanced, and the number of colonies was noticeably reduced. The MIC values of R-CDs against E. coli, P. fluorescens, P. fragi, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes were 4.0 mg/mL, respectively, and the MIC effects against A. sobria, H. alvei, and S. marcescens were 2.0 mg/mL.

A. sobria, the specific spoilage organism in aquatic products, possesses strong spoilage capabilities and can secrete extracellular enzymes, siderophores, and other spoilage factors, resulting in an unpleasant odor [38]. Therefore, A. sobria was used as the model bacterium in this study, and the impact of R-CDs on A. sobria was investigated through scanning electron microscopy. As depicted in Fig. 2b, before R-CD treatment, A. sobria exhibited a rod-shaped morphology with clear structural edges and a complete cell wall. However, after 6 h of treatment with different concentrations of R-CDs, A. sobria underwent significant changes; the bacterial morphology was severely damaged, with noticeable bacterial shrinkage and the extrusion of a large amount of cellular content. CLSM was employed to stain live and dead bacteria of R-CDs to further assess the antibacterial activity of CDs. The resulting findings are presented in Fig. 2b. The redder the staining cell and the darker the color, the higher the antibacterial ability. Bacteria treated with R-CDs exhibited a pronounced increase in red fluorescence compared to A. sobria in the normal saline group. Moreover, an inverse relationship was noted between the R-CD concentrations and the presence of green fluorescence. Notably, the image of A. sobria treated with 4 mg/mL R-CDs displayed minimal living cells, suggesting the potential of R-CDs to effectively exert an antibacterial effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect of R-CDs on A. sobria
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibitory impact of R-CDs on each developmental stage of A. sobria was quantitatively assessed using absorption spectrophotometry. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the inhibitory effect of R-CDs is dose-dependent and time-dependent in inhibiting A. sobria growth. The inhibitory effect becomes more pronounced with increasing R-CD concentration. Studies have established that the growth cycle of bacteria consists of four distinct periods: lag phase, logarithmic phase, stable phase, and decline phase [39]. A. sobria in the blank group rapidly entered the logarithmic phase after a 4-h lag phase. After 12 h, the growth was relatively slow, suggesting that A. sobria entered a stable period. Meanwhile, the optical density (OD) value of A. sobria in the stable phase with 1 mg/mL R-CDs decreased significantly, indicating inhibited growth. After treatment with 2 mg/mL and 4 mg/mL R-CDs, the growth of A. sobria was negligible. With the increase in R-CD concentration, there may be more opportunities for R-CDs to contact A. sobria and act on the cell wall to disrupt the cell structure, thereby effectively exerting antibacterial effects.

Ultra-small R-CDs can penetrate the interior of bacteria and play an antibacterial role [40]. The variation in the MIC of R-CDs for the tested bacteria may be attributed to differences in the charge on the surface of the bacteria, leading to variations in electrostatic interaction and, consequently, different antibacterial effects.

The inhibitory mechanism of A. sobria by R-CDs remains unclear. To evaluate the mechanisms underlying the antibacterial effects of R-CDs, we analyzed changes in cell membrane permeability, intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), protein and DNA content, as well as the levels of alkaline phosphatase (AKP) and Na+ K+ -ATPase. Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) can be cleaved into DCFH by intracellular non-specific esterase. DCFH can be further oxidized to fluorescent DCF in the presence of ROS. Consequently, DCF fluorescence intensity serves as a measurable indicator of ROS levels. The fluorescence intensity of A. sobria treated with 2 mg/mL R-CDs increased by 2.6 times, as depicted in Fig. 3c. This increase may be attributed to the inhibitory effect of R-CDs on bacterial antioxidant enzymes, leading to elevated intracellular ROS levels. This finding aligns with the study conducted by Li et al., indicating that synthesized CDs could restrain the activity of SOD and CAT enzymes in E. coli and elevate intracellular ROS levels [26].

Furthermore, the release of cell components can be used to assess the integrity of the cell membrane. Accumulating evidence suggests that small ions, such as potassium and phosphate, tend to leak initially, followed by macromolecules, such as proteins and nucleotides [41]. As depicted in Fig. S3, the DNA bands in the control group appeared bright, whereas the brightness of the bands in the R-CDs treatment group gradually diminished with increasing R-CD concentration. This finding indicates a reduction in intracellular DNA, potentially due to the damage to the bacterial cell membrane following R-CDs treatment, leading to the leakage of macromolecular DNA. This phenomenon aligns with the results of Tejwan et al. [42], which demonstrated that synthesized CDs could result in DNA leakage in cells after disrupting the cell membrane. Proteins play a crucial role in the physiological processes of bacterial cells, exerting a direct influence on both their growth and metabolism. The protein content of the control group increased with the prolongation of culture time, likely related to the growth and metabolism of bacteria during culture. The intracellular protein content decreased rapidly within 0–3 h, with an average decrease rate of 21.8 %, indicating severe damage to the bacterial cell membrane and significant protein leakage. The decrease rate of protein content within 3–12 h slowed down and tended to be gentle, with an average decrease rate of 9.6 %. It is plausible that R-CDs may enter the cells and affect protein synthesis, gradually decreasing intracellular protein content within 6–12 h (Fig. 3d) (P < 0.05). Compared to the control group, the intracellular AKP enzyme content and Na+ K+ -ATPase content decreased as the culture time prolonged and the R-CD concentrations increased (Fig. 3e, f) (P < 0.05). Simultaneously, we assessed the permeability and integrity of the bacterial cell membrane by directly measuring the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein diacetate in bacterial cells. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of the untreated group was the highest, decreasing after R-CD treatment, and the fluorescence intensity was inversely proportional to the concentration of added R-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the 4 mg/mL treatment group was the lowest, indicating severe damage to the bacteria and an increase in the cell membrane permeability.

These results suggest that R-CDs can elevate intracellular ROS levels, leading to DNA and protein leakage. Additionally, R-CDs can rapidly disrupt the cell wall and cell membrane of bacteria, increase cell membrane permeability, and thereby inhibit the survival of A. sobria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant capacity assessed by chemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidants play a crucial role in neutralizing free radicals within our bodies [43]. The antioxidant activity of R-CDs was evaluated using the ABTS and DPPH methods, known for their ease, speed, and cost-effectiveness. ABTS served as a free radical initiator, oxidized by potassium sulfate to generate blue green ABTS-free radicals. Upon adding antioxidants, ABTS-free radicals were reduced, resulting in a lighter system color. The antioxidant capacity of R-CDs was assessed by measuring absorbance at 734 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, R-CDs exhibited high antioxidant activity in ABTS aqueous solution. At a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL, the free radical-scavenging rate of R-CDs reached 99.36 %, comparable to that of ascorbic acid. DPPH, a conjugated molecule with three aromatic rings containing a lone pair of electrons, changes color from deep violet to bright yellow when reduced by accepting a hydrogen radical in the presence of antioxidants [44]. Using vitamin C (VC) as the control, we determined the reduction of free radicals by measuring absorbance at 517 nm. Fig. 4b demonstrates that the antioxidant ability of R-CDs positively correlates with its concentration. When the concentration exceeded 0.5 mg/mL, the DPPH-free radical-scavenging rate surpassed 90 % and stabilized, approaching the DPPH-free radical-scavenging rate of VC. The superior antioxidant activity of R-CDs may be attributed to the involvement of its surface functional groups in scavenging free radicals, resulting in the generation of more stable products in the ABTS and DPPH assays [45]. Studies have revealed that the antioxidant activity of CDs originates from the hydroxyl group of their surface functional group [46]. Both ABST· and DPPH· radicals are quenched when the hydroxyl group is converted to H·. When compared with other biomass R-CDs (Table 1 ), R-CDs displayed relatively more significant antibacterial and antioxidant activities, indicating their potential use as an antioxidant biomaterial.

The total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of R-CDs was studied through the fluorescence recovery after the photobleaching (FRAP) method. The FRAP standard curve regression equation was y = 1.4376 x + 0.1811, with R2 = 0.9913. As shown in Fig. 4c, the TAC gradually increased within the range of 0.5–3.0 mg/mL as the concentration of R-CDs increased. Notably, at a concentration of 3.0 mg/mL, the TAC of R-CDs closely approached that of VC. The FRAP method relies on the reduction reaction between Fe (TPTZ)2(III) and antioxidants, indicating the reducing capability for ferric ions, which is not directly relevant to physiological antioxidant activity.

Yolk lipoprotein, as the main source of lecithin, is often employed to study the inhibitory effect of antioxidants on lipid peroxidation of lecithin [52]. The primary mechanism involves the oxidation and decomposition of yolk lipoprotein to MDA, or its analogs induced by ferrous ions. The inhibition ability was determined by the TBA method, and the results are presented in Fig. 4d, Under the same conditions, the inhibition rate of R-CDs on lipid peroxidation was higher than that of VC. Upon reaching a concentration of 3.0 mg/mL, the inhibition rate of R-CDs reached approximately 73 %, whereas that of VC was only 46 %. These findings indicate that R-CDs possess a superior ability to inhibit lipid peroxidation of yolk lipoprotein than VC.

Excessive reactive oxygen species can lead to oxidative damage, contributing to the development of diseases [53]. The results from Section 3.3.1 have already confirmed the chemical antioxidant ability of R-CDs. Here, we further examined the antioxidant activity and ROS elimination ability of R-CDs in HEK-293 cells, establishing an oxidative damage model by exposing the cells to H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5a, the cell viability in the group treated with 200.0 μmol/L H2O2 was comparable to that of the control group, indicating minimal impact on cell viability at this concentration. However, when cells were exposed to H2O2 concentrations ranging from 300.0 to 900.0 μmol/L, their viability significantly deviated from that of the control group, decreasing with increasing H2O2 concentration. A cell proliferation rate in the range of 50 %–60 % was chosen as the model, as cells within this range exhibited characteristics of oxidative stress damage, effectively indicating the impact of the sample on the model [54,55]. Considering that the concentration of 800.0 μmol/L resulted in approximately 50 % cell damage, we selected this concentration as the working concentration in the model based on cell viability and cytotoxicity.

Satisfactory biocompatibility is crucial for the in vitro application of R-CDs. To verify the biosafety of R-CDs and determine the concentration for subsequent experiments, we conducted cytotoxicity tests. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the viability of HEK-293 cells remained above 95 % even when the concentration of R-CDs reached 600 μg/mL, showing no significant difference compared to the control group. These results provide evidence that R-CDs exhibit superior biosafety. With the non-cytotoxic nature of R-CDs confirmed, we used H2O2 to simulate ROS-induced damage in HEK-293 cells and assessed the protective impact of R-CDs against oxidative damage. In Fig. 5c, the cell viability in the model group was 58.1 %, and it significantly increased after treatment with R-CDs. Across R-CD concentrations ranging from 100 to 600 μg/mL, there was a corresponding 10 % increase in cell viability. These results indicate that R-CDs exhibit a distinct protective effect against oxidative damage in cells. Additionally, Fig. S4 indicates the hemolysis of rat red blood cells following co-culture for 1 h. It can be observed that CDs have minimal effect on red blood cells. Notably, at a concentration of 5.0 mg/mL, the hemolysis rate was only 1.74 %, indicating that R-CDs have good biocompatibility and could be used as a safe and effective antibacterial agent.

The overproduction of ROS disrupts the balance between oxidation and antioxidation in cells, leading to a state of oxidative stress [56]. Intracellular SOD primarily functions to eliminate intracellular ·O2 −. Studies have shown that reduced SOD activity results in the rapid accumulation of intracellular ·O2 −, leading to increased lipid peroxidation. Meanwhile, MDA and GSH act as crucial lipid oxidation products and hydrogen donors to scavenge free radicals, indirectly reflecting intracellular damage caused by oxidative stress [57,58]. We evaluated the protective mechanism of R-CDs by investigating the levels of several cellular oxidative stress indicators, including MDA, SOD, and GSH. As depicted in Fig. 5d-f, the concentration of R-CDs incrementally increased the levels of SOD and GSH in the cells, while decreasing MDA levels. The SOD and GSH levels in cells treated with 600 μg/mL R-CDs were higher than those in the injury group, showing a 1.62- and 1.48-fold increase, respectively. The MDA level in cells treated with 600 μg/mL R-CDs decreased (Fig. 5f), reaching approximately 75 % of the injury group. These results reveal that R-CDs can effectively reduce the damage caused by H2O2 to cells and exhibit a certain protective effect on oxidative damaged cells. This phenomenon may arise from the capacity of R-CDs to activate endogenous antioxidant systems, comprising antioxidants and related enzymes, to mitigate cell damage induced by H2O2.

In the preceding chapters, we investigated the in vitro antibacterial mechanism, in vitro antioxidant activity, and protective mechanism of R-CDs on oxidatively damaged cells. R-CDs destabilize cell structures, enhance cell membrane permeability, and facilitate the efflux of macromolecules, such as DNA and proteins, from cells. Additionally, R-CDs could increase the intracellular ROS content of bacteria and cause oxidative damage to bacteria. The oxidative damage model was constructed with HEK-293 cells. The contents of antioxidant enzymes SOD and GSH in the R-CDs treatment group were 1.62 and 1.48 times higher than those of the damage group, and the peroxidation product MDA was 0.75 times that of the damage group, suggesting that R-CDs effectively reduce the damage caused by H2O2 to cells and exhibit a protective effect on cells undergoing oxidative damage. These findings validate the potential of R-CDs as an antibacterial agent and their utility in enhancing the function of active packaging films (Fig. 7a).

The S/CS/R-CD films were subsequently synthesized through hydrogen bonding without using any crosslinking agents or catalysts (Fig. 1a). To investigate the effect of R-CDs on the S/CS film, the S/CS films were examined via FTIR spectroscopy. The results are shown in Fig. 6a. In the pure S/CS film, the characteristic peak of S/CS was observed at 3276 cm−1, representing the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH. Furthermore, the bands at 2924 and 2879 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of CH [59,60]. The absorption peak at 1647 cm−1 represented the stretching of the amide I band [61]. Moreover, the peaks at 1560, 1152, and 1020 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of NH, the stretching vibration of the pyran ring, and the stretching vibration of the CO bond, respectively [62,63]. No new absorption peak was observed in the infrared spectrum of the film after adding R-CDs, suggesting that no new chemical bond was produced. Nevertheless, in the R-CD-loaded S/CS film, the tensile vibration of -OH and -NH shifted to 3279 and 3281 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the peak associated with the NH bending vibration (1560 cm−1) decreased in intensity, indicating that R-CDs were added to the film by binding with the amino hydrogen bond of S/CS [64].

The phase compositions of S/CS-based polymers were determined using XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. 6b. The XRD images of the S/CS, S/CS/R-CD, and R-CD films showed two diffraction peaks at 20°. Compared with the XRD patterns of the pure S/CS film, the S/CS/R-CD films and R-CDs showed similar diffraction patterns. This observation can be attributed to the proximity of the diffraction peak of R-CDs of the S/CS film and the low concentration of the R-CDs, indicating a uniform distribution of R-CDs within the polymer matrix. The homogeneous distribution of CDs in composite films can be attributed to the amorphous and non-crystalline nature of the CDs [65].

The thickness of the film is closely related to the film-forming material. As can be seen from Table 2 , there was no significant difference in the thickness between the films, indicating that small-sized CDs are uniformly distributed in the polymer matrix. Sul [23] et al. added carbon quantum dots to chitosan/gelatin packaging film and found that the carbon quantum dots did not significantly affect the thickness of the film.

Improving the mechanical properties of the film is essential to ensure that it can withstand external stress and maintain its integrity during the food packaging process. The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) reflect the strength and toughness of the film. Fig. 6c depicts the TS and EB of the film. As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the TS of the film became stronger with increasing R-CDs, which may signify the enhancement of the R-CDs to the film matrix through physical interaction. Past studies have demonstrated that the increase of nanoparticles decreases the mechanical properties of the film, mainly because of the uneven dispersion and the uneven stress transfer of nanoparticles in the film. In this study, when the concentration of R-CDs increased to 1.5 %, the TS did not decrease, indicating that the R-CDs had a good dispersion in the film and excellent compatibility with the film matrix. The enhancement effect of R-CDs on the film can limit the movement of the film matrix. Therefore, EB decreases slightly when the concentration of R-CDs increases.

Fig. 6d exhibits the water vapor permeability (WVP) of the composite films with different R-CDs additions, and the WVP of the S/CS film is 6.36 × 10−11 g/m × s × Pa. The WVP of S/CS film was related to the concentration of R-CDs. When 1.5 % of R-CDs was added, it decreased to 5.18 × 10−11 g/m × s × Pa. Although R-CDs are hydrophilic, the uniform distribution of the polymer matrix creates a tortuous path for water vapor diffusion, which makes molecular exchange more difficult. Meanwhile, R-CDs produce more hydrogen bonds with the film matrix, and the hydrophilic groups in the film are reduced, thereby reducing the WVP of the composite film [66]. The increased moisture resistance of the film is expected to extend the shelf life of packaged products via preventing moisture transfer between food and the outer packaging material.

Light adversely affects the quality and nutrition of food, especially UV light, mostly due to oxidation of the oil in food, resulting in rancidity. To further understand the barrier properties of R-CDs to UV light, UV–vis scanning was performed in the transmission mode. As shown in Fig. 6e, the maximum UV transmittance of starch/chitosan film in the entire UV region (200–400 nm) was 58.37 %. When the concentration of R-CDs increased, the transmittance of the composite film gradually decreased in the UV region. The maximum transmittance of the composite film with R-CDs addition of 0.5 %, 1.0 %, and 1.5 % in the UV region decreased to 49.33 %, 46.13 %, and 39.29 %, respectively. With the addition of 1.5 % R-CDs, the transmittance of the composite film was almost 0 in the range of 0–280 nm. The results suggest that the S/CS/R-CDs film had a high UV-shielding ability, hence the film can be used as a food packaging material to protect food from UV damage.

As shown in Fig. 6f, when compared with the S/CS film, the swelling ratio of the composite film was significantly reduced after the addition of R-CDs. The R-CDs added to the polymer network may limit the movement of the polymer chain through hydrogen bond interaction, which reduces the expansion or dissolution of the composite film.

SEM observation of the films (Fig. 6g) displayed that the surface of S/SC was rough, with observable cracks. With an increase in the concentration of R-CDs, the surface of S/CS/R-CDs became smoother, and no cracks were observed. This observation can be attributed to the addition of nanomaterials, which increased the connection between starch and chitosan. The infrared results showed that the tensile vibration of OH and NH shifted to 3279 cm−1 and 3281 cm−1, respectively, which indicated that the addition of R-CDs increased the formation of new hydrogen bonds, which indirectly indicated an increase in the mechanical strength of S/CS/R-CDs films. The energy spectrum of the SEM (Fig. 6h) of the S/CS/R-CDs film showed that the C, N, and O elements were evenly distributed, indicating that the components (S, SC, and R-CDs) were uniformly distributed in the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antioxidant properties of S/CS/R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial packaging protects food against microbial contamination and increases its shelf life. The antibacterial effect of S/CS-based films against A. sobria was evaluated, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. The pure S/CS film showed no antimicrobial activity. The S/CS/R-CD films showed antibacterial activity, and the antibacterial effect increased as the concentration of R-CDs. This can be attributed to the gradual release of R-CDs loaded onto the film after reaching the solution. Furthermore, R-CDs were in complete contact with bacteria, thereby damaging the bacterial structure and outflow of macromolecular substances in the cytoplasm. Additionally, reactive oxygen species led to oxidative stress, which resulted in bacterial death. Thus, the prepared film can be used for practical purposes such as producing antibacterial packaging materials.

The antioxidant activity of the S/CS film was determined via a chemical method (Fig. S6). The pure S/CS film showed some antioxidant activity, and the ABTS and DPPH scavenging efficacies were 12.1 % and 10.95 %, respectively. This can be attributed to the interaction between hydroxyl groups of S/CS and free radicals. Furthermore, the antioxidant activity of R-CD-loaded films was greater than that of the pure S/CS. The addition of R-CDs increased the DPPH and ABTS-free radical-scavenging ability of the S/CS film by 84.96 % and 98.16 %, respectively. The superior antioxidant activity of S/CS/R-CDs can be attributed to the involvement of hydroxyl groups in free radical scavenging. Furthermore, the ABTS radical-scavenging effect of S/CS/R-CDs film outperformed that of DPPH. This can be attributed to the water solubility of ABTS. The hydrophilic characteristic of R-CDs allowed for complete dissolution and interaction with ABTS in aqueous solution, which improved results. These films showed remarkable antioxidant activity. Therefore, they can be used as food packaging materials aimed at increasing the shelf life of food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of S/CS/R-CD films in salmon fillet preservation
ce:section.ce:para: The preservation effectiveness of the S/CS/R-CDs film was investigated through sensory evaluation and the spoilage index of salmon fillets [67]. The change in sensory quality directly reflects the freshness of salmon fillets. The morphology of muscle tissues of salmon fillets during cold storage is shown in Fig. 7b, c. The quality of the salmon fillets degraded gradually, which was evidenced by the darkening of salmon fillets, the gradual disappearance of white lines, and an increase in the exudation of water and oil from the surface. On day 0, all salmon fillets meat was bright in color, showed clear muscle structure, and was devoid of any odor. Furthermore, the muscle tissue fibers showed firmness and tightness. By day 6, salmon fillets in the control group showed a dimming effect and a gradual loosening of muscle tissue fibers. However, salmon fillets in the other groups showed no such change, indicating a potential preservation effect of the films on the salmon fillets. On day 8, the salmon fillets in the PE group began to darken, developed white spots, and emitted a smell of rotten salmon fillets. This is because PE film did not possess antioxidant properties. Due to protein decomposition, large gaps appeared in muscle tissue fibers on day 10. However, the use of S/CS and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 films effectively delayed the spoilage of salmon fillets, and salmon fillets exhibited spoilage characteristics on day 10. This delay can be attributed to the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of CD films, which delayed the spoilage of salmon fillets and increased their shelf life by approximately 4 days.

Salmon fillets are an extremely perishable product, and improper storage can result in rapid autolysis and microbial changes, which consequently reduces its edibility. The sensory score of salmon fillets stored at 4 °C for 10 days is depicted in Fig. S7. With the increasing storage time, the sensory quality of the salmon fillets decreased. A decrease in the sensory score of the blank control group was the fastest, which indicated that under the same storage time, spoilage was the most serious in the control group, followed by the PE treatment, S/CS, and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 groups. In the S/CS film treatment group, the antibacterial and antioxidant effects of chitosan reduced the number of colonies of salmon fillets decreased, weakened the degree of lipid oxidation, and delayed the spoilage. R-CD addition further improved the antibacterial and antioxidant capacities, and the spoilage of salmon fillets was delayed.

The rapid proliferation of spoilage-related and pathogenic bacteria deteriorates seafood. Thus, the freshness of salmon fillets can be assessed by evaluating microbiological indicators, and TVC is one of those crucial indicators of the shelf life of aquatic products [68]. A TVC value exceeding 6 log CFU/g implies salmon fillet degradation. Herein, the TVC value was 3.85 log CFU/g at the beginning of storage (Fig. 7d) (P < 0.05). During storage, the total number of colonies in each group increased gradually, and the control group exhibited the fastest increase. During the first 4 days of storage, the TVC values of all groups were below 6 log CFU/g. However, by day 4, the total number of colonies in the control and PE treatment groups reached the critical threshold of 6 log CFU/g for raw fish, and the S/CS and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film treatment groups showed deterioration signs at days 6 and 8, respectively. These results highlight that the notable shelf-life extension of 4 days was achieved by implementing the S/CS film with R-CDs.

pH is a crucial indicator for evaluating the freshness of salmon fillets. Herein, the pH of the S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 composite film group was lower (Fig. 7f) (P < 0.05). Initially, the pH decreased in all detection groups and then increased over time. The decrease in pH was attributed to the increase in anaerobic glycogen glycolysis and ATPase activity in salmon fillets, resulting in the accumulation of lactic and phosphoric acids. With the increasing salmon fillet storage time, the proteins in it produced alkaline nitrogen-containing compounds, such as ammonia and amines, because of the action of bacteria and enzymes, thereby correspondingly increasing the pH [6]. Spoilage causes a pH change at 6–8 [69]. The pH of the S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film treatment group was the lowest at the end of storage, with the smallest fluctuation range observed during storage (Fig. 7f). This trend was potentially attributed to R-CD release from the film, which inhibited the growth of spoilage-related bacteria and thus delayed salmon fillets spoilage.

TVB-N was primarily a product of protein decomposition in fish and an indicator of freshness. Changes in TVB-N contents over time are presented in Fig. 7e, and an overall increasing trend was observed. After 10 days, TVB-N contents in the control and PE groups surpassed the acceptable limit, thus indicating the inedibility of the salmon fillets. Specifically, on day 10, TVB-N contents in salmon fillets wrapped in the S/CS and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 films were 27.37 mg/100 g and 23.45 mg/100 g, respectively, which indicated the capacity of the two films to inhibit TVB-N generation. Moreover, spoilage-related bacteria decomposed proteins into TVB-N; however, the antibacterial activity of the S/CS film delayed protein decomposition by microorganisms. Moreover, R-CD addition improved the antibacterial activity and antioxidant potential of the film, thus eventually inhibiting oxidation and reducing TVB-N production in the salmon fillets [70]. Consequently, the decreased TVB-N content in the S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film treatment group was attributed to the dual role of R-CDs as an antibacterial and antioxidant.

Lipids in fish meat are prone to oxidation, leading to a decline in nutritional quality and fish fillet spoilage. The initial TBA value of the fillets was 0.047 mg MDA/kg (Fig. 7g), and it increased almost linearly in all groups over time. The TBA values of the control and PE treatment groups were comparable, probably because neither group contained antioxidants. On day 10, the TBA values of the S/CS and S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film treatment groups were significantly lower than those of the control group, and this difference was attributed to the antioxidant properties of chitosan, which inhibited lipid oxidation. R-CD addition further decreased the TBA value, thereby indicating that the S/CS/R-CDs-1.5 film effectively delayed the lipid oxidation rate of the salmon fillets.

In summary, radish residue R-CDs with ultra-small size and good water solubility were prepared using the one-pot hydrothermal method in the present study. These carbon dots exhibited low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Additionally, R-CDs exhibited outstanding antibacterial properties. These properties were attributed to their ability to disrupt the integrity of bacterial cell walls and membranes, which causes the leakage of macromolecules such as proteins, DNA, and enzymes (AKP and ATPase) while simultaneously increasing intracellular levels of ROS. R-CDs exerted antioxidant effects by increasing the protein levels of antioxidant enzymes. Additionally, the increased level of the free radical scavenger GSH and the decreased level of the lipid oxidation product MDA in vitro indirectly reflected the good antioxidant damage ability of R-CDs. Furthermore, the storage experiments showed that the S/CS film containing R-CDs effectively inhibited microorganism proliferation, mitigated salmon fillets quality deterioration, and extended salmon fillets shelf life by 4 days. In conclusion, this study shows that R-CDs can be used as nano-antibacterial agents with dual roles of antibacterial and antioxidant functions. Additionally, although the large-scale production of these R-CDs warrants further analysis, the present findings provide a substantial basis for the future application of R-CDs in seafood preservation using S/CS films.
